ON ANIMALS
FRAGMENTS OF A BYZANTINE PARAPHRASE OF
AN ANIMAL-BOOK OF THE 57TH CENTURY A.D.
TRANSLATION, COMMENTARY AND INTRODUCTION
BY
Prof. F. S. BODENHEIMER, D.Sc. and A. RABINOWITZ, M.A.
D'HISTOIRE DES SCIENCES EDITEURS
12 Rue Colbert — PARIS ile 33a-35, Oude Rijn — LEIDEN

div.head: 1. THE HISTORICAL AND LITERARY BACKGROUND
div.p: When Timotheus the Gazaean wrote his books, Gaza, the old Philistine town, had been a hellenized city for nearly 800 years.
After having been captured by Alexander the Great in 332 B.C., it soon developed into one of the most prosperous—and therefore coveted—cities of the East.
Owing to its geographical and strategical position, Gaza was the ‘key to Egypt’; to possess it was essential for every actual or potential ruler of Syria.
Small wonder that it changed hands repeatedly until the Romans took possession, and even then suffered heavily through the vicissitudes of the political situation.
In 66 A.D. it was (probably only partly) destroyed by the Jews rising against the Romans.
However, the town recovered quickly and soon regained its position as centre of Greek civilization.

Yet another factor was to no little extent responsible for the importance of the town: Commerce.— There is good evidence that as far back as in the 18th century B.C., the town had served as an emporium for import and export, the main customers being Arabia and Egypt. Lying in the heart of an agricultural country where many Caravan-routes met, Gaza owed its prosperity to the trade in grain, wine, silver and, above all, spices. The rulers of the city and country changed, but the economic needs of the adjacent territories remained the same throughout the centuries, and so did Gaza’s rôle as an entrepôt. Even the foundation of Alexandria could not do much to shake the importance of Gaza, because the latter’s interests were directed eastwards whilst Alexandria looked to the sea, to the West. It is true that Gaza, too, had its port (called Μωτάτου), but it always played a merely subsidiary rôle in the commercial life of the town.

It is only natural that a place where people of so many races and nations met, became in course of time an intellectual centre too. Greek culture had been received enthusiastically here, as well as in near Ascalon, and the seeds had fallen on fertile ground.
To give a full account of the rôle played by Gaza and Gazaeans in the evolution of later Greek Literature and Science, would be a task

far exceeding the frame set to this introduction. Moreover, much philological and historical research-work has yet to be done before an adequate treatment of this subject can be attempted. Suffice it therefore, to sketch here the broad lines of development.

The intellectual life of Gaza and the Surrounding cities and territories seems to have always been under the influence of Alexandria the undisputed centre of Greek culture during the Hellenistic period. This dependence is illustrated by the fact that famous Gazaean
Although we know of Gazaean and Ascalon scholars and philosophers already in the 1st century B.C., it was not until the 4th
thetoricians enjoyed also certain Privileges and immunities and ranked among the most prominent citizens of their respective cities
Procopius of Gaza 1 (465-528 A.D.). Although a layman, he led
INTRODUCTION 3
A few of his speeches have come down to us and they, together with his numerous letters, form the main source for our knowledge of Byzantine Gaza.

His eminent pupil and successor, Cuorictus, ‘was only a rhetorician and not at all interested in theological questions. However, he spoke and taught pure Attic which could no longer: ‘be studied at Athens
, and he had cultivated an elaborate and refined style such as was never reached again by any of his successors or by any Byzantine author. Two of CHoricrus’ speeches contin’ very detailed descriptions of Gazaean churches, so detailed ‘indéed, that these harangues are much better known among experts of Byzantine art than among classical scholars. Perhaps his most famous speech was that delivered in defence of the stage-playérs ‘whose existence was threatened by an Imperial decree 2 (526 -A.D.) forbidding theatrical performances throughout the Empire. — Bae Ts j

Although the School of Gaza laid the main stréss ‘on oratory, we also hear of noted jurisprudents, philosophers ‘and- doctors who belonged to this circle. As a curiosity we may merition that even a Latin grammarian, a certain Hiertus, chose Gaza as his ‘sphere of action.

Our picture of the Gazaean School would be incomplete, were we to omit to mention its other ‘faculty’, comprising the mathematicians and men of science. Unfortunately, our sources tell us but little about this aspect of Gazaean intellectual life, ‘but’ from scraps of surviving evidence we gain the impression that this branch of learning was by no means neglected. Thus we recall that Eutocius (ca. 500 A.D.), the celebrated commentator of ARCHIMEDES, was a native of Ascalon though he studied his science at Miletus: A striking display of mathematical terms correctly used in the writing of Choricius shows that mathematics was taught in Gaza, perhaps even by Eutocius who probably returned to his own country after completing his studies abroad.

That mechanical and technical knowledge was highly developed there, is proven by a Gazaean treatise (formerly ascribed to Courtius) describing minutely a complicated clock and also by the poetical description by Ioannes of Gaza (ca. 640 A.D.) of the church of St. Sophia in Constantinople. Let us add that a passage in Choricius is our only authority for the use of luminous letters in ancient times.
There is one instance on record that an Athenian came to Gaza to study Attic.
The subject of zoology and its main representative in Gaza Timotheus, will be treated in a separate chapter.
It is noteworthy that the zenith of the Gazaean School (ca. 500 A.D.)
Christianity had established itself—not without hard struggle—
in southern Palestine about a century earlier, the Church had made
great efforts to win the favour of the ma.
their inmost hearts. Splendid churches were built, public buildings
a basilica and (at least) two theatres. Besides, the town was renowned
for the pompous feasts that were periodically celebrated here and
attracted large numbers of visitors from far and near. For Gaza had
way in order to enjoy this “stranger-loving” city.

itself always most benevolent towards the flourishing city and its
Evtocta (401-460 A.D.) to a new built Gazaean church. The cit

celebrating their birthdays, etc. with great splendour.
To sum up, we may say that all our sources for the history of Gaza
in early Byzantine times (i.e. until a.p. 530 approximately) show us
the town as a prosperous city and a centre of learning, a town “which
could admittedly not compete with the beauty, wealth and splendour
of Antioch, Tyre or Caesarea, but which was inferi
virtue of its citizens”
me possess but few details about the life of Timotheus.
Besides the fact that he was a pupil of Horapotto (see p. 2 above) almost everything we know of our author is summed up in a short article by the Byzantine Lexicographer Surpas (ca. 950 A.D.)
follows: pI Tans as
* This epithet is given to Gaza by a 6th century pilgrim Cuorictus, VIII 33 ff, (ed. Foerster-Richtsteig). Sermon: Tey
Timotheus the Gazaean, a learned man, lived at the time of the Emperor Anastasius to whom he addressed a tragedy on the so called ‘chrysargyron’-tax.
He also wrote epically four books on the quadrupeds of India and Egypt, and (those) which Libya rears, and on exotic as well as portentous birds, and on snakes.
On analysing the above passage we get the following facts about Timotheus:

a) He was a Gazaean: This statement is unanimously confirmed by all sources that mention Timotheus. To this external testimony we can add a piece of internal evidence, namely chapter 24, 2 which clearly suggests that he had himself seen the animals and their guardian passing through Gaza.
b) He lived at the time of the Emperor Anastasius 1. This fact, too, is borne out by external and internal evidence; cp. the same chapter.

c) He wrote a tragedy on the ‘chrysargyron’-tax and addressed it to the Emperor Anastasius: Modern readers may find it rather unusual to submit in the form of a tragedy a memorandum on a burdensome tax, yet this drama—together with representations made by some monks from Jerusalem—induced the Emperor to abolish * the hated tax (A.D. 501). Thus the ‘tragedy’ had a happy end. Most modern scholars think that this ‘tragedy’ was simply a speech, or perhaps a poetical address, because real tragedy 4 had died out centuries earlier. One may ask in wonder why Timotheus who, as a scholar, had no actual interest in opposing a business-tax, intervened in this matter. There is little doubt that he did so at the request of his fellow-citizens who deemed a sophist to be the fit person to address the Emperor, just as the Jerusalemites had put their trust in clerical intercessors. For sophists and clerics stood uppermost on the social ladder.

d) He wrote in metrical language four books on exotic animals. This was apparently Timotheus’ most important work because most later Byzantine sources know him only as zoologist. The title of the book is quoted in different forms such as ‘On the Indian animals’, ‘On the quadrupeds and their innate wonderful qualities’ or shortly stories
In modern Greek ‘tragoody’ means any song or melodrama.—A part of this ‘tragedy’ seems to be preserved in a manuscript found in the library of the monastery on Mt. Athos.

teachers. It may therefore be concluded that these ‘Tales from Timotheus’ as we may call them served not only the general reader, but were also used as a school-book; this, in turn, became apparently so popular that it superseded the original work and consigned it to oblivion until the real Timotheus eventually became lost.
How the paraphrase of Timotheus’ book ‘On animals’ came down to us will be told in chapter four of this introduction.

div.head: 3. THE PLACE OF TIMOTHEUS AMONG THE ANCIENT ZOOLOGISTS
a) THE MAIN SOURCES FROM THE ZOOLOGICAL POINT OF VIEW
div.p: Perhaps the most surprising result of an analysis of the sources of Timotheus’ book ‘On animals’, as far as they can be judged from parallels in older writers, is that undoubtedly either a full text or a very good excerpt of the Historia Animalium of ARISTOTLE—but no other of ARISTOTLE’s zoological writings—was well known to Timotheus. Exactly one third of the parallels, most of them indicating by their wording the direct use of the Historia Animalium, are referable to this book. This percentage is considerably larger than is found in any other animal book of late antiquity. Many sentences in Timotheus are almost quotations from the Hist. Anim., and once even the name of ARISTOTLE is mentioned. It is interesting to note the distribution of the paralleis over the nine books of the Hist. Anim.:
Books of H.A. I Il Il IV Vv VI VII VIIiIix No. of parallels ) | 2 ‘ > 4 to a8 in Timotheus EE
Percentage of
no. of his paral- /
lels to all nine
books

Almost one third of the parallels are from the eighth and a similar proportion from the ninth (pseudo-Aristotelian) book, another fifth from the sixth book, and the remainder (18.7%) from four other books, while two books furnish no parallel. Now, books I-V contain the morphology and anatomy of the animals, which topics obviously found little favour in the eyes of Timotheus. Book VII discusses human reproduction, whilst no part of the Timothean fragments

Animalium are conspicuously prominent in the parallels. And this is
easily understandable, as Timotheus of Gaza himself was no zoolo-
— but a collector and compiler of interesting notes about animals
such as are found in PLINY or in the Physiologus, in AELIAN or in the
Pseudo-aristotelian De Mirabilibus Auscultationibus, only better

known €pic poem from the early 3rd century A.D., on the big game of the Middle East, has recently been made easily accessible by, the edition and translation of A. W. Mair in Loeb’s Classical Library
The thirty-three Parallels from that book show that Timotheus hi

really intimate acquaintance with that magnificent collection of sea-lore is certain. It can Safely be assumed that in the lost parts sea-life, the Halieutica must have Played a dominant role a siike.

Third m importance as source for Timotheus (thirty-two parallels) are the animal Stories of AELIAN (circa A.D. 150). It seems almost certain that Timotheus knew this amusing collection of animal fables
have not withstood the weathering of the centuries,
The same may be said about two other sources. PLutarcy (circa
A.D. 100), mainly in his Moralia, viz. De Sollertia Animalium and De Iside et Osivide, yields a number of parallels which may very well be due to direct quotation by Timotheus.

This also holds good for the parallels from the Geoponica (compiled in the 6th century A.D. from various older authors) which may be not direct quotations but drawn from other books of the ‘sympathy’-type initiated by the Physica of Botos Demoxritos (2nd century B.C.).

WELLMANN (see below, Bibliography B) has properly pointed out that the Physica is the first of a series of typical animal books of later antiquity about the antipathies and sympathies of animals, as well as their pharmaceutical and magic qualities.
Chapters 7, 10 and 17, 2 of our paraphrase give typical illustrations of what might have been found in these books, which are mainly preserved in the Geoponica.
Timotheus has six close parallels to this latter work.

Parallels to the Historia Naturalis of Puixy the Elder (died a.D. 79), at least nine in number, are unconvincing. We do not assume that Timotheus was actually acquainted with this work. It is much more probable that identical or similar sources were used. The trend of Pity is often rather divergent from the version given by Timotheus. There is no parallel between these two writers that could not equally have been based on any one of a number of other sources available to Timotheus. Further parallels from Priny could easily be adduced, but we have no indication that the Historia Naturalis was actually used by Timotheus nor does it appear that he drew upon any other Latin sources.

Really astonishing is the scarcity of parallels (three only) with the older Physiologi. It is almost impossible that a learned Palestinian writer of 500 4.p. should not have been fully familiar with their contents. We can only assume that either by some scientific snobbery Timotheus disdained to draw from this source or that the School of Gaza was in general opposed to the theological and moralizing insertions and tendencies with which these books were tainted. The few actual parallels from the Phystologus could all have been taken from earlier, pagan and Christian writers, such as HoRAPOLLO

(late 5th century a.D.). This is, for instance, true of the very peculiar story on the use of the oryx as a ‘calendar-animal’ (chapter 23, 3) which is found in the Smyrna Physiologus, but also in older Alexandrian writers. As the parallels are so scarce, we are inclined to assume that they were all taken from the older Alexandrian literature.

b) THE ANIMAL LIST OF TIMOTHEUS
Sumpas (see P. 4 above) tells us that the animal book of Timotheus was originally an epic poem in four books. The prose paraphrase which
~~» We are reasonably certain that
Se ko of “terrestrial quadrupeds” (mammals, reptiles and
ae a oa as ve = the ostrich is contained in our fragment. Of the
foe te H introductory chapters, one was certainly devoted to the lion. other two chapters may have been of a general and intro-
INTRODUCTION If

ductory character, or possibly devoted to man or bear, or one chapter to each of these last two. The animal list of the fragment does not contain: The birds (including the bats), the fishes (including the invertebrates and tammals of the water; the seal (ch. 57a) may be a fragment of the book on aquatic animals), any of the terrestrial invertebrates, such as insects, spiders, snails, earthworms, etc.—The creatures of the air and of the waters were certainly described each in a special book. The same is quite probable for the insects and their like. If this surmise be correct, we have before us the major part of the first book of Timotheus’ ‘On animals’, the other three being wanting entirely except the fragment beginning with the seal.

These conclusions are suggestive, but not proven. The terrestrial quadrupeds form the bulk of the animal lists in all early animal books. And their share in the book is the greater, the smaller the zoological knowledge of the writer. It is thus quite possible that the fragments here translated contain the larger part of two of the four books.

The reader may point out that the primitive major taxonomic division is not that of ARISTOTLE. But he must recall that ARISTOTLE himself has never given any précis of his animal system. This system was only reconstituted about hundred years ago by prolonged and painstaking analyses carried out by scholars like J. B. MEYER, AUBERT, WIMMER, etc. And the most decisive conclusions come from the other zoological books of ARISTOTLE, not alone from the Historia Animalium.

Thus it is entirely reasonable to assume that somebody perfectly familiar with the Historia Animalium may be equally unfamiliar with the animal system of ArisToTLE. And this obviously is the case with Timotheus of Gaza who was certainly no brilliant zoologist.

The group of the terrestrial quadrupeds in the 52 chapters of the Timotheus fragment contain 58 animals which are distributed over the modern system as follows (the name of each animal is followed by the number of the chapter dealing with that animal):

Besides, there are mentioned 57 species of marine fish, mammals, birds and molluscs of which, however, not all can be ascribed with certainty to Timotheus.

There is hardly an original observation or remark in our fragment of Timotheus, and where it seems that there is one, it is merely the record of a curiosity by a historiographer, such as the remark on the giraffe travelling from Aqaba to Istanbul through Gaza.

That Timotheus himself was utterly unfamiliar with many of the animals treated by him is obvious from a number of facts. Suffice it to mention that the leopard appears under three different names in three different chapters, and that the striated and the spotted hyenas are described in two chapters as distant as 4 and 50.

A number of exotic, rare animals are mentioned, such as gnu, zebra, giraffe etc., but obviously the knowledge of Timotheus about them is not more penetrating than that about the common animals of his country.

He was more concerned with animal lore than with natural history. Not direct observation of animals, but books about them constituted his sources. Yet, therein he differed by no means from the overwhelming majority of naturalists from the epigones of Aristotle to the Renaissance.

The extant paraphrase of Timotheus’ poem ‘On animals’ which was written about A.D. 500, most probably in Southern Palestine (cp. ch. 24, 2), represents an independent type of animal book: It is not a book on animal sympathies and properties in the style of the Physica of BoLos DEMoKRITOs; it is not a mere collection of anecdotes for pleasant reading as the books of AELIAN; there is no dealing with animal psychology as in one of PLUTARCH’s treatises; it is—at least in the present paraphrase—not a verbose poem splendidly depicting nature and intermixed with lengthy and plastic mythological tales, as are the epics of the Opprans; and certainly it is not a weighty textbook of zoology in the sense of the zoological writings of ARIsTOTLE. All these books served, however, as sources to Timotheus who composed from abstracts of them as well as of other, less important books, his animal compendium; the aim of his work might perhaps best be described by a subtitle, as ‘“What the educated philosopher (the equivalent for the Victorian gentleman) should know about animals’.
The Historia Animalium of ARIsTOTLE, the animal stories of AELIAN and the Cynegetica of Oppian offer the majority of those

The great German scientist and polyhistor K. GESNER (1516-1565),

in his book De quadrupedibus p. 743 (published 1603). There he gave a Latin translation of TimoTHEvs’ chapter 56 (On the arctomys). Of course, he did not know who was the author of the treatise—this ‘was not discovered until approximately roo years ago (see page 16) —so he simply called him ‘a certain Greek of a more recent age’. The MS. itself had been lent to GESNER to assist him in his editio princeps of AELIAN.

After that, the excerpts are not mentioned during two centuries. It was in 1800 that C. F. MATTHAEI (1763-1822) turned his attention to the Anonymus Augustanus (as the author was called by J. G. SCHNEIDER *)and published a short article on him in the Literarischer ‘Anzeiger of that year, p. 1055. Yet, MaTTHaEr did not rest at this, but eventually produced the editio princeps of TimoTHEUS 3.

However, MATTHAEI’s edition met with little luck. It had been printed in Moscow under the patronage of a noble Russian family, and few copies had been sold when Napoleon’s Army entered the Russian capital which soon after was consigned to the flames. We have reason to believe that almost the entire edition of MATTHAEI’s book was destroyed by that fire. Thus the Napoleonic wars which had brought in their wake the removal of the Codex Augustanus to Munich, now again interfered with TrmoTHEUs’ treatise. Habent sua fata libellz.
It is only natural that MATTHAEI fixed the date of his anonymus

somewhere in the 2nd half of the 12th century, since he had no guide for his purpose except
MonomaAcuus is mentioned as contemporary (cp.
note 8). Yet, the word ‘that’ (6t1) which appears invariably at the beginning of each section of our treatise, might have given to MATTHAEI a hint that this MS. represented a paraphrase of, or an epitome of excerpts from, some older author on a zoological topic.
In 1843, CRAMER published a number of previously unedited MSS. of the Bodleian library in Oxford (Anecdota Oxoniensia), and among

them the 11th century Codex Baroccianus 1 which contains the chapters 7 (in part), 29-32, 35 and 42-49 of Matruart’s Anonymus. Moreover, the anonymity was now lifted, because there was a heading which clearly indicated that the chapters were “of Timotheus the Gazaean scholar (from his book) on quadrupeds and their innate wonderful qualities, Poetically put by him into beautiful words” (Analecta Oxoniensia tom. IV P. 263 ff.).
A few years after our text in the Augsburg MS. had been identified,

Moritz Haupt (1808-1874) who was then professor at the university of Berlin, decided to publish it. For this purpose he had emended the text and had furnished it with short explanatory notes and parallel loci, most of which have been embodied in the notes to the present
however, bibliographical Scrutiny revealed that MATTHAE!'s book had meanwhile become a ‘rarissima avis’, so Haupt reconsidered his decision and published the Timotheus excerpts in the German
Philological journal Hermes vol. 3 Pp. I ff. It is the text 2 of this edition that has been translated by us.

(No. 2422), by H. Grarr in the Bulletin de l’académie impériale des Sciences de St.-Petersbourg, 1864, p. 21 ff. These fragments too, though not all of them can with certainty be ascribed to TIMOTHEUs of Gaza, appear at the end of our translation (pp. 49-51).

div.head: ON THE HYENA (tata)
div.p: That year by year it becomes alternately male and female.
That it has sharp and thick hair.
That it does not bow its neck since it has one bone instead of the vertebra (1. column).
That it mates with a wolf and bears the so-called lonewolf which does not join (the other wolves) but lives alone, preying on men and animals.
That it steals decomposing bodies from the graves that it sees by night as by day.
That (by) vomiting it induces the dogs to come forth and thus it catches them.
That it flatters the dogs and playing with (or on) their muzzle it chokes them.
That, walking in the moonlight, if there is a dog on a roof, it catches his shade from below (and thus) it drags the dog down from above.
That when it finds a sleeping (man) it stoops under his head, and seizing his neck, drinks the blood.

ON THE HEDGEHOG (ἐχῖνος ὁ ἐπίγειος)
1 That the hedgehog (when) fightings kills the snake and piercing it with its spines it cuts it into pieces.
4 Hedgehog defeats the fox by fight, (the latter) being le to overpower the spines 5.

ee hedgehog foretelis the winds that are about to blow. For hedging up its earth (in the direction) from where the wind is — i ee i ide in the direction of the (wind) whi it opens it from the other side in : 5 already ceased (to blow)’. Therefore, anyone that has ae this is held to foreknow the winds when he announces which (winds) going to blow.
me That it comes in summertime and shaking the grapes from the vines and wallowing (on the soil) it empales the grapes and $0 it goes to its earth (just) as it is, pluming itself with the πέλεκυ
5 That its gall ground with wax causes abortion
That the wolf when it gets the first look on somebody dumbs him dumb and speechless for some hours.
That it is by nature hostile to sheep and preying upon them.
That when it has eaten immoderately blocks its throat.
That wolf is so hostile to sheep that (it) often (happens) that when as αἴ τι is sown on a lyre, the other sheep-strings do not sound ἀνέσει being struck do not tinkle.
That the year is called ‘wolf-year’ (λύκειος) from the λύκος.
That the meat of sheep (which were) killed by the wolf is pleasant to the taste, but clothes made from their (i.e. of those sheep) fleeces are not.
That there are many kinds of wolves,
That if two wolves Prey together on a sheep and tear it with their teeth 8, the division is equal. ° muneer
That the wolf when it is ill, eats earth 9,

E has the parrot as friend like the roe 4 (which has) the partridges*, like the deers 13 (which have) the francolins 14, like the horses (which have) the bustards}, like the goats when they bath at water (courses) (have) the sargue-fish 2.
That the wolf has no vertebra, but (only) one bone, like the lion and the hyena *. Therefore their course is straight.
That if someone carries a tooth of it, he does not fear a wolf,
and a horse carrying same (i.e. a wolf’s tooth) becomes very swift.
That the wolf fears the squill* as the lion (fears) the prinos 5
and the pard® (fears) the cornel shrub’.
That anyone holding a toad’s bone may take® the wolf along.
That a wolf eats iron and stones®. It hides, however, at the time of the dog-star until (the time) when the air cools down.
That the porcupine has the size of a wolf at the most 11:
That it has on its back instead of hairs a sort of spines and big prickles of which the women having them gilded !2 make pins for their curls.
That it shoots them (i.e. the prickles) off?8 when fleeing and kills a man or often a hound 4.
That in the war (waged by the Titans) against Kronos and Zeus,
beasts originated from the blood of the Titans all over the world.’

div.head: ON THE GRIFFIN
div.p: That the griffin has the breast and even the upper (parts) of a horse, the beak and wings of a bird, but the tail and back of a lion.
That the griffin is caught by men, and (of) quivers and (of) its claws big cups.

That they harness o (load), and the griffin which is strong (enough) fastens its claws around it; and being unable to lift such a weight, a man is hidden under such a wagon, and when it gets caught, he jumps up and burns its wings.

They xen to a wagon and its feathers they make load it with a heavy to carry off even an Ox, these (i.e. the claws) it cannot get free, and that a griffin which is strong (enough) fastens its claws around it; and being unable to lift such a weight, it remains caught; and that a man is hidden under such a wagon, and when it gets caught, he jumps up and burns its wings.
That it dies of hunger when it gets old because its beak grows crooked with time and hence does not allow it to partake of food.
That the zebra resembles the wild asses. It is a very swift animal.
i hy colour.
That it has a tattooed skin and an as
That they were harnessed and driven by charioteers in the theatre
J llous spectacle.
of Rome, and they presented a marve ;
4 That the Imperial princesss took part in processions on such
a chariot.
That there are two kinds of pards, the big ones having a short...
tail 4. _
7 That the pard has a sting on its tail like (the one) on (the tail)
of the lion, and a carnivorous tongue ;
That the pards were the female disciples of Dionysos°, and that
Pentheus insulted Dionysos, and they prayed that they tight pee
beasts and rend Pentheus in pieces, and this they becamed? and kille
the man.
That the pards like wine§ and hence they are caught by the hunters; great quantities of wine being provided, (the pards) get drunken
and fall asleep. .
That they artfully catch the wild goats ® (by) blocking the cross
oads with ordure and urine.
That when they are suffering from the sickness called ‘pardalianchos’!° they eat ordure and recover.

That the wild goat is ἀτύαξ or ἀτύαξ as if it were flying Ty swift and moves about on the cliffs
ἀτύαξ is another name for Felis pardus.

p.quote: ἐν τῷ πρὸς Αἰλιανόν Commentary to AELIAN. Variae historiae 5, 40 (H) :
τὸ δὲ Ἀκόσ Capra aegagrus, is identical with the Greek ibex

div.p: it jumps from the cliffs into the depth, down from the peaks, and does not hurt itself, except if it happens to tumble down, (in which case) it is dashed to pieces.

That the wild goat cherishes its father, and when he can no (longer) move with age}, it brings him fodder and water in its mouth and nourishes him, just as the alkyons ἀλκυών on the sea (nourish) the kerylos κέρυλος, their father.
That the wild goat loves its young more (than other animals do theirs), so that, when they are caught, it follows them and wants to share their lot.
That it has a hole in its horn (through which it draws air and breath as (if it were leading) to the snout); but if someone stops this (hole) it dies.
That the goats in Crete (when) being hit by an arrow eat a plant called dittany “ and immediately they drop the arrow and recover.
That the goats of the Cappadocians ® are shorn (and yield) the goat-wool.
That the gazelle ® (when) being pursued stops for a short while and makes water, (then) runs quicker 1° and escapes.
That the gazelles and the partridges are friends " of each other; hence they are caught by the hunters through (the presence of) each other.
αἰθύη i.e. kingfishers.

γρυπός; the MS. reads here ‘χύρξος’ which name is nowhere else found. The emendation to ‘κέρυλος’ by Mattuaet is justified by numerous parallels e.g. ANTIGONIUS CarRysTIUS, Mirabilia 27; AEL. De an. 5, 48.

Arist. H. A. 8, 28 p. 606 a 16 points out that in Lycia goats are shorn as sheep elsewhere. Possibly an early reference to Angora goats.

3 That wherever one returns from the troop to ease itself, there move the others too. And they join the wild asses.
ON THE HARE (Aaxyeés)
That the doe bears, a hare in her womb, becomes pregnant again and moreover goes to (have) intercourse.
That the hare becomes once male, once female.
That the does when pregnant are slow as if they were walking on mud. In spring the hares are fatter.
That they, being cowards, keep their eyes open while they are asleep.
That the colour of their skins resembles the earth on which they dwell.
That it, being sharp-sighted, flees the eagle.
That there is no hare in Ithaca & to the sea and perishes.
That the tortoise (χελώνη) 7

That the tortoise eats the fleas.
That when someone throws it on its back, it cannot turn about
on land and equally not in the sea.
When dried up by the sun on the shore, it can no more
dive back into the sea.
ON THE SQUIRREL (σκίουρος)
That the squirrel is overshadowed by its tail like the peacock
and hence it is called ‘shadow-tail’.
ON THE βουνασός (bounasos)
That the bounasos is a big animal in Paeonia, having the form
and appearance of a bull. But its hide covers a whole house.
That because of the hairs over its eyes, it looks not straight on,
but sideways.
That the colour of its skin resembles the earth and the places
it dwells.
That it has horns (which are) harmless because (they are) bent
or hot and ill smelling ordure of the belly, it
burns the hunting hounds and men, because it shoots
(its dung) far off, and by that very ordure it feeds its young.
That the onager is a very swift animal !4, and there is often a

That when it is pursued, by kicking with (its) feet it shoots stones and wards off the pursuers.
3 That the ostrich 3, the (so) called ‘sparrow-camel’ 4, does the same.
4 That the ostrich 5 is omnivorous and it often eats iron and dissolves it.
That the ostrich and the wild ass are similar as regards the carrying stones,
That the beisa is a horned animal, and (there is) another ‘Oryx’, a worm (ὄστρακον) (which looks) as if (it were) horned.
That the rivers Nile and Hydaspes breed beisas.
That the Egyptian priests revere the beisas since they announce beforehand the dog(-star) Sirius by remarkable sneezing.
That they have always a scout, and when he warns them by sneezing that an animal is approaching, they immediately form lines of battle and guard the weaker ones (among them).
Henceforth simply called ὀτρουθιάς without the addition ‘the big’.
In their stomachs (?). The Byzantine zoologist Manuel Pures (14th century) in his Versus de animalium proprietate, line 150, reports that stones are found in the dissected body of an ostrich.
Oryx leucoryx (LSJ). Arist. H. A. 2,1 p. 499 b 20 mentions the oryx as cloven hoofed and with but one horn.
That the giraffe accepts the approaching lion! or (any) other animal with its horns and kills him, and it happens (sometimes) that it itself is killed.
That the giraffe is an Indian animal; and it is born from the interifferent animals.
sit @ tian dealing in Indian products and hailing from Aila passed through Gaza, bringing two giraffes and an elephant to Emperor Anastasius.

This was seen in our times too: for also to the Emperor Constantine Monomachus were these two animals (i.e. giraffe and elephant) brought from India, and were at each opportunity shown to the people as a marvel, in the theatre of Constantinople.
That the elephant has no knees.
That it has a trunk with which it does everything as with a hand.
That it has big teeth or horns.
That singing women enchant it and lead it to the hunt, in the same manner as a virgin also leads out the unicorn.
Cp. Oppian. Cyneg. 2, 464 ff.

That they are law-abiding and moderate, and have a leader and pass by rivers carrying their young, and heat a site pee Sl dies abate (the consequences of) the.
That each of them has only one spouse like the most moderate.

That when one commits adultery among them, it is killed by the children, and allow a dog to bark.
That the dogs see dreams, as says Aristotle.
That towards the rising of Sirius, dogs become rabid, but they are healed when bathed.
That they have the dragons as enemies and they often kill them.
That a dog eats a (certain) herb and a cornflower, it empties its bellies and (voids) the worms.
That the dragons have precious stones in their eyes. But the Indians bewitching them put them to sleep and kill them and take away the stones. But often the hunters are drawn by them.

div.head: ON THE HORSE (ἵππος)
div.p: 1 That from different peoples there are different horses as regards their virtue.
2 That a mare desiring intercourse, when she is shorn 2 τὰς τρίχας of her hairs (ie. tail and mane), is quiet as if she were stripped.

3 That the mucous humour of the mare is the so-called ‘Hippomanes’ 3; licking this from the forehead(s) of the new born foals, the dams love them. But also women who deal in drugs and potions use this (substance) for love-(potions).
4 That horses have a bone in their hearts and some cows 4 (have it) too.
5 That the stallion lives 35 years but the mare 30 5; by the way, (there has been) a horse (which) lived 75 years.
That horses cover and copulate as long as they live except two years of their youth and of their old age §,
That the mares, when other dams die, suckle their foals (i.e. foals of the dead mares) 7.
That when one wants a dappled horse, he draws (a picture of) such a horse near a spring, and the mare seeing the picture bears such (a dappled foal) %. And this is also (the case) with doves.
And in Lacedaemon in the chambers of pregnant women they draw (pictures of) beautiful youths.
That the horse does not cover his dam or sister.
That two fight for the female, and the victorious (one) keeps her as his own and intimate, and (the stallions) being enemies recognize the voice of each other at very long (distance).
Ir That the horses see dreams ”, but among men there are some who never dream, and when they once in their lives see (a dream), they (may) expect disaster or death.
That they (i.e. the horses) fear camels unless they are used to them.
Equus caballus (domestic).
That bulls fight strenuously against each other for the females.
That when the cowherd rubs against his hand the udder of a cow that needs to be covered, and adding some sweat takes fast hold of the bulls’ nostrils, he stops them from fight(ing).
That the cows have two udders and four teats.
That the blood of bulls curdles near the heart of those who drink it, and kills (them) 5. .
That the Erythrean kine shake their horns like the(ir) ears &,
That the copulation of cattle like that of deer is carried out (by the bull) scaling’ (the cow), because of the sinewy (parts) 8.
That there is a four-horned bull who has sexual commerce with horses, and from him issue the so-called kallaioi horses.
That there is (a sort of) cattle called ‘grazing backwards’ from their grazing backwards !° which they are forced to do because of the length of their horns.
That there is a (kind of) horned cattle called Bison which have, as it were, a carnivorous tongue 1. They originate from the country Bistonis 22,
That the boubalos resembles partly an ox and partly the Libyan deer.
That there is another boubalos beyond the Alps near the river Rhine *. But this boubalos is white and murderous.
When it is caught 4 and then set free, it goes back to its own forest ®. This does also the smooth lobster in the sea ®.
That the mules being born from different species do not bear, and live a long time ?.

That a she-mule in Athens lived 80 years 8, distinguishing itself at work when the temple of Athena was built. The people (of Athens) passed a decree that this (mule) should not be kept away from the cornfield(s) nor from any other necessity for its subsistence.
That the urine has a cleaning effect ® on the she-mules as (has) the menstruous blood on women, (and) the he-mule smelling them (ie. the females) gets prematurely old.
That asses in cold places soon perish 11,
That among the Hyperboreans they sacrifice asses ® to Apollo
Bubalis mauretanica (LSJ).
? This sub-chapter is not indicated under a special heading in the Greek text.
* The transalpine boubalos is the aurochs (Bos primigenius),
* This refers apparently again to the ordinary boubalos.
5 Cp. Oppran. Cyneg. 2, 306 ff.
® davaxdc. Cp. Oprran. Halieut. i, 263 ff.
7 Codex B adds here: ‘that the mare then quickly admits the donkey to sexual commerce, when one shears her hairs (i.e. mane). Cp. p. 18, 9 above.
* Cp. Arist. H. A. 6, 24 p. 577b 29.
® Cp. ibid. p. 587 a 2.
1° Cp. ibid. 8, 28 p. 606 b 4; De generatione animalium 2,8 p. 748 a 24.

11 This passage may have been taken from CLEMENS ALEXANDRINUS, Protrepticus 2, 29 (H). The Hyperboreans were a fabulous people in the extreme North. It is noteworthy that tombs of horses and asses buried during the Hyksos period (ca. 1750-1500 B.C.) have been found during excavations in Gaza,

“1 tvpa¢. Haupt attributes this statement to an error which has sprung from a misunderstood Egyptian custom: According to Prutarcu, De Iside et Osiride c. 30 p. 362, the evil god Set-Typhon was believed to resemble an

That camels could carry a big load if they were not accustomed to be loaded while kneeling down
and (then) to get up with their loads on.

That they in the South cover bigger distances
than (any) other beast and by night they recognise their way by the stars. Hence the Indians (mounted) on them travel eastwards and steal the golden dust from the Indian ants
.
That whilst the ants stay inside their hills during the heat, the Indians steal their gold dust.
That they (ic. the Indians) harness together three camels of which two are males, and in the middle a female that has dropped recently; and when they have loaded (the gold dust) they ride (away)
1 unaic. Cp. Arist. H. A. 8, 25 p. 605 a 16.
a (see p. 19 n. 9) (p. 17) adduces a late Arab parallel.
3 Equus asinus (domestic).
s = ee Cp. Arist. H. A. 9,1 p. 609a 31. According to LSJ this bird may be identified with the linnet.

Cp. ARIST. ibid. 5, 31 p. 5574 15. This statement was transmitted through the ages on the authority of ARISTOTLE, and it was a most courageous eau of F, Rep! to give an illustration of a ‘‘Pediculus asini” in his famous book ‘De generatione insectorum’ (Florence 1668).
e 6 Cp. ARIST. 2, I p. 499 b 12 and 19. The ‘Indian ass’ is possibly the Indian rhinoceros whose horn was reported to neutralise any poison.
7 Camelus dromedarius.
8 Literally: ‘sitting down’.
® Literally: ‘march more than...’.
10 Cp. HERODOTUS 3, 102 ff.
Pp. 21

fleeing from the ants lest they may be caught; and if the males cannot follow, the female alone holds out and drags them (i.e. the males) along, since she is in a hurry because of her young.
5 That the Bactrian camels are two-humped.
6 That people castrate the males lest they may be unruly in war 1,
7 That the she-camel brings forth one young 7 only each time and never twins.
8 That the male when he sees a human being ® whilst he is mating, rages to kill (the onlooker).

div.head: ON THE BOAR (niga)
div.p: 1 That the boar has such a hot tooth that a hair touched (by it) burns, and through his bite the hairs of the dog fall off.
2 That Libya which produces all sorts of animals, produces no wild swine.
3 That the rut of the boar is (so) wild that the fleeing sow is often killed by him.
4 That the milk of sows does not curdle; but if a human being sucks from a sow, it becomes leprous.

5 That when a boar is at war with another one, he withdraws into a wood and keeps constantly training, and having hardened his skin he comes (forth) and fighting incessantly he kills and (sometimes) is killed.
6 That the boar alone of (all) animals becomes stronger when losing his genitals.
That the sheep in Libya drop thrice a year at regular intervals.
That in Libya which is very hot and rich in pastures, the sheep become horned very quickly, and they drop incessantly, and milk is wanting at all there.
That in Libya there is a kind of sheep, wild but silly and easily caught; but their wool is useless.
That in Scythia, because of the cold winds, the sheep mostly bear but once at a time and have no horns.
That in Crete there are sheep with four horns.
That all sheep that are spotted have the blood vessels under their tongues spotted (too).

That the soubos is, like a sheep, yellow and smooth; and it is liked by the fish (because) it is amphibious, and swimming it eats them while they are gathering round it. Yet it itself is often caught by the fishers.
That the cat which is usually called by us (by its) Roman (name) ‘katta’, is said to have sprung from the interbreeding of pard(s) in Libya.
And the female is said to be more harlot-like and to fawn upon the male during intercourse.
That the kids watch each other when they lie down to sleep, and
Cp. Arist. H. A. 8, 28 p. 606a 18. oes aca Lerner
— suggests the reading ‘all the year through’ (84 κατὰ ἐνιαυτόν) instead of ‘at all there’ (84 πανταχῇ ἐνταῦθα).
7 The lion is correct (Oppian, Keller). ; ;
$ ee oe ‘Galen, 2, 382-444. An unidentified animal, either sheep or deer.
° Felis catus (domestic). ; . .
10 ie ph ie structure of this sentence being objectionable, it is probable that the whole sentence is corrupt.
11 Cp. Arist. H. A. 5,2 p. 5404 II. a a,

a8 εἰς weighs is read ‘into intercourse’ (εἰς συνουσίαν) instead of the transmitted ‘during intercourse’ (ἐν συνουσίᾳ).
l. 30
P. 23

div.head: ON THE MARTEN
div.p: 1 That the so-called ‘ἴκτις’ (i.e. marten) which we are wont to call ‘αἴλουρος’ (i.e. cat) creeps under the wall and devours the domestic fowls; but when it falls, it blows itself up and filling its skin with air it is not harmed.
2 If someone puts rue 3 on (the place) where the fowls are accustomed to be in the courtyard, (then) the beast (viz. the marten) does not approach them (so) quickly.
3 That the genitals of this beast are bony 4, and they are applied for curing the bladder in cases of difficult micturition.
That there is in Libya a common kind of mice and hares,
That the mice in Libya do not drink,
That mice often grow out of the late rain,
That mice bite sleeping persons, and one (mouse) bites the finger,
the other makes it swell and renders it unsensible.
That they dip their tails into lamps and draw the oil and lick it when they cannot put their heads in.

div.head: ON THE WEASEL (yaar)
div.p: That the weasel drops (its young) through the ear, but—according to others—through the mouth.
That the weasel, when it fights against the snake, eats rue α0 for the snake dreads this (herb). Moreover, the snakes that eat each other, are deathbringing. But (the snake) dreads human saliva,
Here is a lacune of approximately four letters,
Mustela foina,
That if shrew-mice bite when pregnant, they kill.
That if the shrew-mouse bites someone on a road used by wagons,
then its bite is healed by the mud from the wagon wheel.
CHAPTER 40—ON THE MOLE-RAT (dondauk)
That the mole rat is a blind weasel which, creeping under earth, eats the roots of plants and dries them up.
It is caught by bait or in traps.
It is said that the molerat is Ehieue i; he was turned into a beast by the anger of Helios and therefore, when it is seen by Helios, the earth no longer receives it back.
CHAPTER 41—ON THE LAND CROCODILE (yepaaios xooxddethoc)
That the (land) crocodile dreads the saffron and thence has its name.
That its dung is useful for the eye.
That it fights with the scorpion and is not harmed provided it eats a certain herb.
CHAPTER 42—ON THE CROCODILE (xpoxé8erA0)
That crocodiles live in two rivers only, in the Nile and in the Hydaspes.
That it is called crocodile, taking the name of the land-crocodile or because it avoids the saffron with fear or because it has a saffron-like skin.
That when they bite, the teeth being fixed opposite each other, they do not get off unless they tear the flesh out.
? poyahat. Refers to species of Sorex and Crocidura. This subchapter is not indicated under a special heading in the Greek text.
2 Cp. Arist. H. A. 8,24 p. 604b 10 ff.

: fecha told in full detail by Oppran. Cyneg. 2, 612 ff.
8 The sun-god of the Greeks.
Varanus griseus.
xpoxog (saffron) + Setdog (afraid).

an Hi inte Djalam, a tributary of the Indus.

1. ro
42 TRANSLATION
4 That only the upper jaw of this animal is moved, not the lower jaw.
5 That iron does not pierce its skin,
6 That one bone runs through from its head to the tail and (therefore) it cannot crook.
7 That a man anointed with crocodile grease jumps into the Nile, and turning it (i.e. the crocodile) on its back by an enchantment, he catches it.
8 That it has a long and sharp tail by the strokes of which it often kills,
9 That it draws a bull into the river and devours him.
10 That if it bites someone, very many cats gather round the bitten man so that they make water upon him, and this they do until they kill him.

Iz That its egg crocodile 3 is like that of a goose, and out of it comes a little crocodile like the land-(crocodile) but in time it grows to (a length of) 10 cubits and more; for as long as it lives it grows. However, it lays 60 4 eggs and has 60 teeth and lives 5 60 years and has 60 muscles and remains 60 days without food and has sexual commerce 60 (times).
12 That when you extract the first tooth from (its) left jaw, you easily abate the ague.
13 That the bird trochilos ® is friendly to him eating the decayed flesh and leeches out of (its) teeth.

14 That the trochilos watches over it when it sleeps 7 and when it (i.e. the trochilos) sees that hunters are approaching or rather the ichneumon (which lives) near the Nile and which is also called ‘enhydros’® or ,Hyllos’®, it (the trochilos) immediately screams and

That the ichneumon (ἰχνεύμων) which is also called ‘enhydros’ or ‘hyllos’ is said to rub itself with mud in order to become slippery and to jump into the mouth of the crocodile and thus to eat up its liver and to kill (it).
ON THE Hippopotamus (ἱπποπόταμος)
That the hippopotamus belongs to the amphibians and has an invulnerable skin as (is) on the elephants. It eats crocodiles and other aquatic monsters.
That the hippopotamus indicates the rising of the Nile; for it walks in the mud up to the level to which the water is going to rise.

That it goes up to the cornfields
® and measures how large an area it is able to eat off, and then retiring slowly, it grazes; for it cannot bend its neck. It happens that, when after drinking it vomits the water on that area, (the place) becomes verdant again.
That by aid of virgins and women it is tamed (when) young.

That it kills its father and covers its mother
’.

That its tooth is helpful for the condition of the stomach, and to the Ethiopians it serves as a remedy against gluttony; for they eat uncooked fish and drink muddy water and they will die if they do not tie the tooth round (themselves).

taken from Theophilus Alexandrinus (ca. 400 A.D.) p. 7, the trochilos is confounded with the hyllos; in the 2nd one which is an extract from Georgius Cedrenus tom. I p. 701 B., the hydris is characterised by a slightly enlarged version of the story told in chapter 43, 1st paragraph. Cp. also Georgius Pisides (ca. 630 A.D.), Hexaemeron 965 ff., adduced by Haupt, Opuscula III, additional note to this passage of the reprinted text of Timotheus.

That the rhinoceros is by its size well matched to the hippopotamus. It lives near the Nile (and) comes out of the ocean.

and with it it often kills an elephant.

3 That the geckos and the ‘galeotai’ and the lizards strip
(themselves) of their skins, that is, when they are old, like the snakes,
and they hibernate like the bears and the crocodiles.
That the wild horse is so stubborn that it can never be tamed or endure bondage; but if this is forced on it, it refuses food and weakened by hunger dies.
That there is a beast named ‘κορκότης’ (i.e. spotted hyena), the size of a wolf; and when the shepherds call their own children

Usually, this animal is identified with the varan. cp. ch. 41.—The Byzantine historian Acominatus Nicetas Choniates (ca. 12004.D.), vol. II p. 417 Bekker, relates that the Emperor Andronicus Comnenus I (1183-1185) ate skinks—which were by experts regarded as inedible—in order to stimulate both his appetite and his lust (Haupt, Opuscula III, additional note to this passage of the reprinted text of TrmoTHEus).

This name is spelt in various ways, such as χροκόττας, χροκόττας, etc. It is Hyaena crocuta.

by name, it imitates the sounds of the names and secretly calls them similarly itself, and outside the village it seizes the children who are deceived by such sounds and run out of the flock, and devours them.
That there is another ‘κορκότης’, of human form.

That it is likely that the brute animals are rational (as may be concluded) from what has been proved concerning the doings of the κορκότης, and the stork, and the lion, the nightingale, the turtle dove, the eagle, the purple coot, the elephant, the bee, the crane, the woodpecker, the swallow, the vulture, the raven, the glaukos, the hippopotamus, the crab, the partridge, the ibis,

div.head: ON THE APE (μτ Syx0<)
div.p: That the ape is a very licentious and wily animal and it has a female of its own.

That a merchant once kept such ape on a ship, and after he had beaten it for doing something wrong, ape stole the gold coins belonging to the man, leapt up to the top of the mast and from there it tossed the gold into the sea and hurled itself down at the same time.
That when fleeing, it jumps from tree to tree and even carries its young on its back.
That being imitative, it is caught by craft when it imitates the man who wants to catch it, and dipping into tar it is caught.
However, it is also caught in another way, by anointing its eyes with a harmful medicament.
That these apes gather with the Indians in the Pepper, deceived by craft and their own inclination to imitate.
That a sick lion hunts it with cunning and devours it; for the meat of the ape benefits his the lion’s health.

p.quote: Cp. Pliny N. H. 8, 107 who, however ‘crocotta’ as an Ethiopian animal that imi

div.p: νoππoυpiov, Phorpyrio,

As the glaukos, an eatable fish of grey colour is—if at all—remarkable only by the fact that it hibernates (Aristotle H. A. 8,1 5 P. 599 b 32 £.), it may perhaps be conjectured that the owl (γλαυξ) which played a prominent rôle in Greek folklore and mythology, was originally mentioned here. However, it is not easy to decide what special sign of intelligence should entitle the owl to admission into the above galaxy of wise animals,
The tail-less Inuus ecaudatus of western North Africa.

p.quote: Cp. Aelian. De anim. 7, 21 example of imitation

div.p: That the sphinxes are quick like birds.
That they drink wine and get drunk.
That there are other small (sphinxes) in the country of the 5, and they are on good terms with men and when they drink they forth the smell of wine like human beings.
That the katobleps is an animal which emits fire from its nostrils.
That the salamander is like a lizard, and passing through fire it cools and extinguishes it.

CHAPTER 54—ON THE BEAVER (κάστορ)
Sedeatt twee rom having a large and wide belly is called ‘castor’
That it is an amphibian and dwells in lairs near waters
That it is bigger than the ichneumon, and with its teeth it fells big trees, eating the roots from below.
That of it the castorian clothes are woven.

That its testicles are essential for various medicines, and beaver is pursued therefore by hounds and men, and knowing the reason tears them (i.e. the testicles) off with its claws, and escapes. But when it, (already) without testicles, is pursued again, it shows that it has no (testicles).
That it often changes its lairs for fear to be hunted. However, it is caught by night; for it dreads the fire and remains motionless and the hunter having a torch gets near and so he can seize it.
That the beaver dwells by the waters and (so does) the satherion and the latax and the enhydris and the sapérion of which the Saperine garments are made.
CHAPTER 55—ON FROGS (Βατράχων)
That frogs often rise into existence out of clouds.
That frogs die when the pond dries up and come to life again when it is filled with water.
That often snakes as (does) the chameleon too.

div.head: ON THE MARMOT (ἀρχοκύων)
div.p: That one marmot watches when the others are feeding and each brings it (i.e. to the watcher) a share of the food; but when that one neglects its watch and anything happens unawares, (then) they kill the watcher ®.

That a thunder-bolt never falls upon the skin of a seal ®; and indeed, for this reason the sailors cover the so-called mastheads of the ships with such hide(s). And similarly hail? will never hurt the vine if you tie round it a short piece of that skin.
That the teeth of the seal make dentition painless for children, but the skin being put round, averts the missile from heaven (i.e. lightning).

That if, after puncturing deep into the hide of a seal you use it as a basket for seeds, and sow the (seeds) which fall through by throwing them thinly on a piece of land so that a small quantity may suffice for a large area, (then) it is impossible that hail will fall in that place during that year; but the neighbours get the hail due to come down on that piece of land.
That in the skiaina ® grow two stones of which the right one is said to cure the right (part) of a mans head, and the left (one) (cures) the left (part).

Arctomys mayrmota or Arctomys bobak. ‘Arctomys’ means literally ‘bearmouse’. Haupt points out that this animal is mentioned by the Roman almanacmaker PoLemius Sitvius (5th century a.p.) chap. 5 and by St. JEROME in the letter to Sunia and Fretela where it deals with Psalm 103, 18. (Vulgata numeration, ie. Psalm 104, 18 in the Hebrew and English versions); there, Jerome remarks that the ‘Sefannim’ (O°35W i.e. conies, Procavia syrica) of the Hebrew original are animals as big as a hedgehog and resembling bears and mice, „whence they are called in Palestine ‘arctomys’”. Haupt also quotes a Bremen codex of the Glossarium Cyrilli where the ‘arcomys’ (this spelling is also adopted by PoLemius Sitvius) is classed between the hedgehog and the porcupine.
Here ends the Augsburg MS.
Phoca monachus.
None of the following titles is indicated in the Greek text.
Now follows the translation of the Parisian MS. Cp. Introduction p. XXI.
Cp. PLutarcu. Symposium 4, 2 p. 664; 5, 9 p. 684e; IonannEs Lypus De mensibus 3, 52; 4,96; De ostentis 45 (H).
Cp. Geoponica i, 14 3. . ;
ὀκταῖνα, a sea fish, probably either Corvina nigra or Umbrina cirrosa.
ON THE KOCHLOS

That a certain oblong kochlos is caught in the sea, and is called ‘πῦρ’ for it resembles a hand in such a position; when its meat is boiled down in water, the water acquires a human mind and voice and cures people suffering from delirium and insanity.
That a snail’s shell tied to the belly of a pregnant woman, keeps her—say the people—until timely childbirth; but it also hinders the child during a woman’s pangs.
That a boiled elops unites opponents on friendship.
That the blood of the kepphos * when applied to the muscles and joints of a man, makes him a good runner.
That they who boil in oil the tender fledgelings of swans, make (for themselves) a great remedy for the muscles.

If you want to fish an octopus *, take olive branches, hang them down into the sea where is a cliff or a rocky beach, and all (the octopuses) will be caught ® by the(se) branches and you just draw them up.
x64205, a shell-fish with a spiral cell used for dying purple: Murex.
zip = fire. GRAFF compares Pin. N. H. 9 § 184. The beginning of this sentence is far from clear in the Greek original,
* This passage allows of an alternative translation:'... the water gets hold of a man’s mind and voice and cures.

According to D’Arcy THompson (see p. 26 n. 10) p. 48, the elops belongs to the sturgeons,
xéngos. Perhaps: Stormy petrel (Thalassidroma pelagica) (LSJ).
® dxtéroug. i.e. Octopus vulgaris. Cp. Oppian. Halieut. 4, 300 ff. Haupt opines that the remaining 16 lines should not be ascribed to Timotheus of Gaza.

p.quote: The MS. reads ‘they will wander about together’ (suprdavicovrat) ; however, GraFr’s emendation to ‘will be caught’ (cuprAaxqcovrat) is plausible.

div.p: If you want to bring the fish of the sea together into one place, wind round a weel to catch a big lot.

If you want to catch with food a fish lying in the sea, take milk of the spurge and of the cyclamen plant and having chopped them up, mix it all by kneading it with flour and throw it as food to the fish wherever you see a lot of them, and eating (the mixture) they are thus conquered and float as though dead and you may collect them with the hand.

That if one shuts the heart of the bird halcyon into a golden capsule together with the skin of the bird, and carries it, (then) he will not be harmed by thunder or lightning. You will have an amulet against thunder if you keep at home a heliotrope on which you have inscribed aphia aphryx.

p.quote: The sense is probably: . . . hiding in the sea. Cp. Opp1an. Halieut. 4, 658 ff.
